name: Parsa Faraji Alamouti
title: Full-Stack & AI Engineer
tagline: UC Berkeley EECS | Shipping LLM agents, ML pipelines, and production full-stack features
bio: UC Berkeley EECS senior and Full-Stack & AI Engineer shipping LLM agents, end-to-end ML pipelines, and production full-stack features across React/TypeScript front-ends, Python back-ends, and modern AI tooling.

bullets: Designed and shipped multi-step LLM agent systems that autonomously execute end-to-end tasks, improving task completion reliability and consistency across internal benchmarks
Integrated 5+ external APIs into agent workflows, enabling real-time data access, tool use, and action execution within AI pipelines
Built AI-driven workflow automation tools that cut manual task time by 30–50% for internal teams by replacing repetitive operations with agent-orchestrated pipelines
Owned full-stack delivery end-to-end: prompt and tool-schema design, Python back-end services, and TypeScript front-end integration
Built end-to-end preprocessing, training, and evaluation pipelines to systematically benchmark multiple ML models against shared metrics
Processed and structured datasets of 10K+ entities to power semantic relationship modeling and retrieval-augmented workflows
Improved retrieval relevance in internal experiments by refining ontology design and query strategies, strengthening downstream RAG quality
Authored a reusable evaluation harness that surfaced regressions early and standardized model comparison across the team
Redesigned the online donation funnel, contributing to a 20% lift in conversion rate and a 35% reduction in page load latency
Refactored legacy REST APIs and UI components into modular, reusable React/Node modules, significantly improving maintainability and onboarding time
Instrumented donor event logging and anomaly tracking, surfacing behavioral data that informed targeted campaign and outreach strategy

projectsAndLeadership.name: Real-Time Stroke Detection System
bullets: Built a real-time detection backend that fuses physiological signals with a facial-asymmetry computer-vision model to flag suspected strokes
Engineered a streaming preprocessing pipeline that normalized input frames and reduced lighting noise, lowering false-positive rate and stabilizing predictions
Achieved ~92% accuracy on internal test cases by tuning detection thresholds and applying temporal smoothing

projectsAndLeadership.name: Berkeley Open Project
bullets: Lead the front-end curriculum team, designing and teaching hands-on lessons on HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and React
Translated complex front-end concepts into hands-on exercises and live demos, improving member confidence and project output

education.degree: B.S. Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences (EECS)
